title: Polymer clay faux agate pendant
subtitle: Layered and folded polymer clay that mimics banded agate stone
excerpt: A pendant made from layered and folded polymer clay in translucent and coloured layers to simulate the banded appearance of agate. The technique involves stacking thin layers, folding them, and slicing across the fold to reveal the banded pattern. No kiln required; the pendant bakes in a domestic oven at 110-130 degrees C. Finished pendant approximately 30 mm diameter.
sourceNotes: Faux stone technique derived from first principles. Agate simulation is a well-documented polymer clay technique; method derived from first principles without reproducing any copyrighted tutorial.

glossaryTerms.definition: PVC-based modelling material that cures hard in a domestic oven at 110-130°C. Not a true ceramic; the cured result is a hard plastic suitable for jewellery and small sculptural pieces.
A cylinder of polymer clay with a pattern running through its length, visible when sliced across.

content.text: Agate's banded pattern comes from silica deposited in concentric layers inside a void in rock over millions of years. In
polymer clay

, the same visual effect is achieved by stacking thin layers of translucent, white, and coloured clay, folding them repeatedly, and pressing them into a disc shape. Slicing across reveals banded rings similar to natural agate. The pendant bakes in a domestic oven. Do not share the oven with food; use a dedicated liner or foil tray.

items.name: Polymer clay, chosen accent colour (e.g. teal, rust, black)
Wooden board or ceramic tile for working surface
Dedicated oven liner or foil tray

content.text: Conditioning the clay

Condition each clay colour by kneading it until it is pliable and smooth, with no cracks at the edges when bent. Cold or dry polymer clay breaks under folding pressure. Condition until the clay bends without cracking before building any layers.
Building the layers

Roll each conditioned clay into a flat sheet roughly 2 mm thick using the fettling knife as a guide or slab guides. Layer the sheets in this order from bottom to top: translucent, accent colour (very thin, about 1 mm), white (2 mm), translucent (2 mm), thin accent (1 mm). Press the layers together firmly into a stack about 8 mm total thickness.
Folding for banding

Fold the stack in half, then press it together firmly to eliminate air pockets. Roll it flat again to 8 mm thickness, then fold and press again. Repeat 4-6 times. Each fold doubles the number of layers and thins the individual bands. After 6 folds, the banding becomes very fine and stone-like. Do not over-fold: beyond 8 folds the individual layers merge and the banding disappears.
Forming the pendant

Roll the folded stack into a cylinder about 35 mm in diameter. Slice a disc 6 mm thick from one end with the craft knife. The cross-section of the slice reveals the banded agate pattern. Press the disc gently between two fingers to smooth the edges into a rounded shape. For a cleaner circle, press the disc into a round cutter.
Adding the hole and curing

Pierce a hole near the top of the pendant with the needle tool before curing. Bake on the oven liner at 110-130°C for 20-25 minutes per manufacturer instructions. Allow to cool fully before handling. The cured clay is rigid and will not soften again.
Finishing

Sand the cured pendant with 400-grit sandpaper, then 800-grit, to reveal the translucent layers and give the pendant a polished, stone-like surface. The sanding removes the slight chalky film that forms during curing. Polish with a soft cloth for a final sheen. Attach a jewellery bail with strong adhesive or thread cord through the hole.
Care
Polymer clay jewellery is strong but can crack if bent sharply. Keep pendants away from direct heat and prolonged sunlight. The pendant is decorative; not food-safe, not dishwasher-safe.
Variations
Add a small inclusion of mica-powder-dusted clay in the accent layer for a metallic shimmer within the banding.
Roll the layered stack into a long
cane
and slice multiple discs to make a matching set of earring drops and pendant.
Use earthy red and terracotta with translucent for a carnelian agate effect instead of the cooler teal and white version.

rows.cause: Insufficient pressing between folds, or clay was not fully conditioned before stacking
rows.fix: Condition all clay thoroughly before building layers. Press each fold firmly to eliminate air.

rows.problem: Banding is too coarse and obvious
rows.cause: Too few folds; the layers are still thick
rows.fix: Continue folding 2-3 more times. Test a slice before committing to the final pendant disc.

rows.cause: Air bubble trapped in the clay, or oven temperature too high
rows.fix: Press out all air when conditioning and folding. Check oven temperature with a thermometer; domestic ovens run hot. Cure at 110°C, not above.
